prompt: Dynamic Programming can solve an MDP only when the agent has:
options: The full transition and reward model
A long stream of sampled episodes but no model
A value-function approximator only
A simulator that can sample next states
explanation: DP is a planning method needing the exact model (transition probabilities and rewards). Learning from sampled episodes is MC/TD, and a pure sampling simulator is not enough for DP's expected updates.

prompt: What is correct about tabular Dynamic Programming?
options: It finds a global solution and is guaranteed to converge to the optimum
It finds a global solution but is not guaranteed to converge
It finds only a local solution but is guaranteed to converge
It finds a local solution and is not guaranteed to converge
explanation: With a full model and a finite tabular state space, DP provably converges to the global optimum. There is no local-optimum trap in the tabular case.

prompt: What are the two alternating steps of policy iteration?
options: Evaluate the policy's values, then act greedily w.r.t. them
Sample full episodes, then average their returns
Roll out random trajectories, then back up the results
Add an exploration bonus, then decay it over sweeps
explanation: Policy iteration alternates evaluation (compute v_π) and improvement (greedy w.r.t. v_π) until stable. Averaging sampled returns is MC; rollouts and bonuses belong to search and bandit methods.

prompt: DP uses 'expected' updates. What does that involve?
options: Summing over all next states, so it needs the transition probabilities
Updating from one sampled transition, so it needs no model
Averaging complete-episode returns, so it needs whole episodes
Updating only the highest-error state, so it needs a priority queue
explanation: An expected update sums over every next state weighted by P(s'|s,a), which requires the actual probabilities. Single-sample updates are TD/Q-learning, return-averaging is MC, and priority queues belong to prioritized sweeping.

prompt: How does value iteration differ from policy iteration?
options: It repeatedly applies the max-based Bellman update, then reads the greedy policy
It fully evaluates the current policy between each improvement step
It learns purely from sampled transitions without a model
It averages Monte Carlo returns to set each state's value
explanation: Value iteration folds improvement into evaluation by applying the Bellman optimality (max) backup until convergence, then extracting the greedy policy. Full evaluation between improvements is policy iteration; both are model-based, not sample-based.

prompt: On the axes policy (on/off-policy) and width (sample/expected), how is the value-iteration backup classified?
options: Off-policy and expected over the dynamics
On-policy and expected over the dynamics
Off-policy and sample over the dynamics
On-policy and sample over the dynamics
explanation: Value iteration uses max over actions (off-policy / greedy) and sums over the full transition distribution (expected). That 'expected' width is what forces a full distributional model.

prompt: Despite its optimality guarantee, why is Dynamic Programming often impractical?
options: Full expected sweeps over all states need the exact model and do not scale
Its convergence to the optimum is not actually guaranteed
It cannot be applied once the rewards are stochastic
It requires sampled episodes that may never terminate
explanation: DP must sweep every state with full expected backups and needs the exact model — both break down as the state space explodes. Its convergence is guaranteed, it handles stochastic rewards, and it uses no sampled episodes.

prompt: Which properties hold for tabular Dynamic Programming?
options: It requires a complete model of the environment
It uses expected (full-distribution) updates
It is guaranteed to converge to the global optimum
It can learn directly from sampled experience without a model
It scales easily to very large state spaces
explanation: DP needs the full model, uses expected backups, and converges to the global optimum. It cannot learn model-free (that is MC/TD), and it scales badly because it sweeps all states (curse of dimensionality).
